facts.text: Medford’s first train station opened in 1843, turning a flat wilderness into a bustling hub on the Long Island Rail Road.
In 1907 the LIRR launched Medford Prosperity Farm, an 80‑acre experimental station proving crops could thrive in the Pine Barrens.
President Theodore Roosevelt stopped at Medford station in August 1910, famously leaping out of a mud‑slicked car to greet the crowd.
For much of its early history, Medford was a rural patch of farms and pine barrens, a landscape described as “scrub oak waste.”
The 1963 Eagle Estates development on Horse Block Road introduced a Blue Jay Market shopping center and a youth athletic field, reshaping the hamlet’s southern edge.
Local radio stations W243ES and WLIM have long served Medford, broadcasting news and music to the community since the mid‑20th century.
